data.title: Polish retailer CCC cuts 2025 profit forecast on revenue shortfall
data.description:
Polish footwear retailer CCC (CCC.WA) late on Wednesday cut its 2025 core profit forecast to between 1.43 billion and 1.47 billion zlotys ($358-$368 million), down from an earlier estimate of 1.7-1.8 billion.

The company blamed the cut on a revenue shortfall in the fourth quarter, with sales coming in about 500 million zlotys below assumptions due to negative like-for-like sales and slower e-commerce growth.

"The published earnings estimates are a strongly negative surprise, especially since the management board revised its full-year expectations in early November" - said Erste Group analyst Piotr Bogusz in a note.

The retailer's gross margin dropped approximately 2 percentage points compared to its projections as it ramped up promotional activity.

One-off events, including inventory write-offs and currency effects, negatively impacted the result by around 65 million zlotys, company said.

Looking to 2026, the retailer predicted improved results driven by a 28% expansion in retail space and anticipated cost leverage, while maintaining its share buyback plans.

data.title: TPFG: Record revenue growth, new income streams, and reduced debt position set up for 2026 expansion
data.description:
Record FY25 organic growth with revenue up 25% to £84.3m and profitability ahead of expectations. Strong performance across franchising, financial services, and licensing, with new income streams and reduced net debt. Confident outlook for 2026 growth.

data.title: Wizz Air reports wider third quarter loss
data.description:
Wizz Air on Thursday reported a wider third quarter operating loss despite recently raising its growth projection for the fiscal year on the back of more Airbus deliveries and returning grounded planes to the sky.

Wizz Air reported an operating loss of 123.9 million euros ($148.48 million) compared to previous corresponding quarter's loss of 75.9 million euros.

Analysts polled by LSEG had projected a loss of 137.95 million euros.

($1 = 0.8344 euros)

data.title: Building AI sure does cost a lot, hope it's worth it
data.description:
BUILDING AI SURE DOES COST A LOT, HOPE IT'S WORTH IT

A look at the day ahead in European and global markets from Wayne Cole.

Who knew building AI would cost so much. Results from Microsoft and Meta showed truly nose-bleeding amounts being spent on capex, with the former splashing out almost $38 billion in the quarter, up two-thirds on the previous year. Meta boosted its capital spending plans for this year by 73% to a range between $115 billion and $135 billion.

Yet investors reacted very differently to those plans, knocking $240 billion off Microsoft's market value while lifting Meta by $140 billion. The Microsoft drop was more than the entire capitalisation of Citigroup, but then banks are so last-century.

Seems Meta's upgrade to its earnings outlook really wowed investors, while the Redmond-based giant struggled to placate concerns that costs would ultimately outstrip profit growth.

Still, one company's costs are another's revenues as shown by a trebling in chip-maker Samsung Electronics' operating profits. Memory chips used to be a boring part of the market, until there weren't enough to go around.

Wednesday's Federal Reserve meeting has also been called boring, though Chair Jerome Powell sounded pretty chipper in his presser. The economy was on a "solid footing" he said several times, with a "clearly improving outlook".

There was "broad support" on the committee for holding rates steady, apart from the two that voted for a cut of course.

The Fed had already done a lot of "normalising" on rates, he added, and policy was now only "somewhat restrictive", or even "loosely neutral". Markets took that as confirmation an April cut was off the table, with June staying at 61% in part because investors assume President Trump will have found a more dovish replacement as chair by then.

Powell, though, would not be drawn on whether he would step down as a Fed governor in May, or stay on until 2028. One reporter wondered why he would want to leave at all given threats to Fed independence, but Powell merely smiled.

Over in currencies, the dollar still looked vulnerable despite Treasury Secretary Bessent insisting the U.S. still has a "strong dollar" policy, no matter what Trump might say.

More meaningful might be the murmurings from EU and ECB officials about the euro's rise on the dollar being harmful to exports and a downside risk for inflation.

Actual intervention from the staid ECB seems highly unlikely, but the Swiss central bank has more form and the flight from the dollar is pushing the franc up broadly, including against the euro.

Indeed, the franc has broken below huge chart support that must have the Swiss worried. Intervention by the SNB can be messy for markets as it usually sells francs for euros, and then spreads some of those euros out among dollars, sterling etc, causing cross-currents in a range of currencies.

Key developments that could influence markets on Thursday:

- Euro zone consumer confidence, business sentiment for January

- Riksbank policy meeting, ECB board member Piero Cipollone speaks

- U.S. November trade data, weekly jobless claims

data.title: Takeda Posts Sharply Higher Quarterly Profit, Raises Annual Guidance
data.description:
(06:32 GMT) *Takeda Pharmaceutical 3Q Net Y103.6B Vs. Net Y23.8B >4502.TO
(06:33 GMT) *Takeda Pharmaceutical 3Q Rev Y1.192T Vs. Y1.144T >4502.TO
(06:34 GMT) *Takeda 3Q Net Profit Missed Y107.9B Expected in Poll of Analysts by Visible Alpha
(06:36 GMT) *Takeda Raises FY Revenue, Net-Profit Views
(06:53 GMT) *Takeda Says Higher FY Revenue Forecast Due to Weaker Yen
(06:56 GMT) Takeda Posts Sharply Higher Quarterly Profit, Raises Annual Guidance

By Kosaku Narioka

Takeda Pharmaceutical reported sharply higher quarterly net profit and raised its annual earnings guidance.

The Japanese drugmaker said Thursday that net profit more than quadrupled to 103.6 billion yen, equivalent to $675.3 million, for the three months ended December.

That was below the estimate of Y107.9 billion in a poll of analysts by data provider Visible Alpha. For the year-ago period, the company reported net profit of Y23.8 billion, which was partly weighed by restructuring expenses.

Third-quarter revenue grew 4.2% from a year earlier to Y1.192 trillion.

For the fiscal year ending March, it projected revenue to decline 1.1% to Y4.530 trillion and net profit to climb 43% to Y154.00 billion. The company previously forecast revenue of Y4.500 trillion and net profit of Y153.00 billion.

Write to Kosaku Narioka at kosaku.narioka@wsj.com
